edges.description: The interatrial communication permits shunting between systemic and pulmonary venous circulations, usually from left to right when pulmonary vascular resistance is not elevated.
edges.target: Left-to-Right Shunting and Right Heart Volume Overload

edges.description: Persistent volume overload can progress to pulmonary arterial hypertension and ventricular dysfunction, especially when defects are diagnosed late.
edges.source: Left-to-Right Shunting and Right Heart Volume Overload

edges.description: Chronic atrial and right-heart volume load promotes electrical remodeling that predisposes to atrial tachyarrhythmias.
edges.source: Left-to-Right Shunting and Right Heart Volume Overload

nodes.description: ASD originates from abnormal formation or deficiency of the interatrial septum or from anatomically related interatrial communications. True septal defects are confined to the oval fossa region, while primum, sinus venosus, and coronary sinus communications have distinct morphologic origins but share atrial-level mixing physiology.

evidence.reference_title: Defining the morphologic phenotypes of atrial septal defects and interatrial communications.
evidence.snippet: True atrial septal defects are limited to deficiencies totally within the confines of the oval fossa and its antero-inferior rim.

evidence.reference_title: Defining the morphologic phenotypes of atrial septal defects and interatrial communications.
evidence.snippet: The unifying physiological feature of all these variants, whether their morphology is that of a defect within the area of the oval fossa, or an opening elsewhere within the atrium, is that mixing of the systemic and pulmonary blood occurs at atrial level.

nodes.description: Right-sided volume overload and atrial chamber enlargement alter atrial electrophysiology, raising the risk of supraventricular tachyarrhythmias and some conduction disorders, particularly with large shunts or late repair.

evidence.reference_title: Arrhythmias and conduction disorders associated with atrial septal defects.
evidence.snippet: This leads to electrical remodeling that may predispose patients to atrial tachyarrhythmias and conduction disorders.

evidence.reference_title: Arrhythmias and conduction disorders associated with atrial septal defects.
evidence.snippet: Risk for arrhythmias is increased with late age of ASD repair, shunt size, other factors such as pulmonary hypertension and comorbid conditions.

nodes.description: A deficiency or ASD-like interatrial communication permits mixing at the atrial level.

evidence.reference_title: Defining the morphologic phenotypes of atrial septal defects and interatrial communications.
evidence.snippet: True atrial septal defects are limited to deficiencies totally within the confines of the oval fossa and its antero-inferior rim.

nodes.description: In most unrepaired ASDs, greater left-sided filling pressure and right ventricular compliance drive left-to-right atrial shunting. The resulting pulmonary overcirculation enlarges right-sided chambers and can produce long-standing volume-overload complications.
nodes.id: Left-to-Right Shunting and Right Heart Volume Overload

evidence.reference_title: Hemodynamic assessment of atrial septal defects.
evidence.snippet: Hemodynamics in children is characterized by left-to-right shunting, dilated right heart structures and normal pulmonary artery pressures (PAP).

evidence.reference_title: Arrhythmias and conduction disorders associated with atrial septal defects.
evidence.snippet: ASDs have left-to-right shunt and primarily right-sided volume overload.

nodes.description: Flow across the atrial communication is usually from left to right in children and in adults without advanced pulmonary vascular disease.

evidence.reference_title: Hemodynamic assessment of atrial septal defects.
evidence.snippet: Hemodynamics in children is characterized by left-to-right shunting, dilated right heart structures and normal pulmonary artery pressures (PAP).

nodes.description: Long-standing unrepaired ASD can produce pulmonary artery hypertension and right or left ventricular dysfunction. These complications are more typical in adults diagnosed late and influence whether closure is safe.

evidence.reference_title: Hemodynamic assessment of atrial septal defects.
evidence.snippet: Patients diagnosed at adult age often present with complications related to long-standing volume overload such as pulmonary artery hypertension and right and left ventricular dysfunction.

nodes.description: Surgical repair remains a valid closure strategy, especially when anatomy is unsuitable for device closure or when the defect is primum, sinus venosus, or coronary sinus type.

evidence.reference_title: Secundum atrial septal defect in adults: a practical review and recent developments.
evidence.snippet: Transcatheter and surgical techniques both provide valid options for ASDII closure, the former being the preferred method.

evidence.reference_title: Secundum atrial septal defect in adults: a practical review and recent developments.
evidence.snippet: With the exception of those with severe and irreversible PAH, closure is beneficial to, and thus indicated in all patients with significant shunts, regardless of age and symptoms.

nodes.description: Device closure is generally preferred for hemodynamically significant secundum ASD when defect size, rims, and pulmonary vascular status are suitable.

evidence.reference_title: Secundum atrial septal defect in adults: a practical review and recent developments.
evidence.snippet: Transcatheter and surgical techniques both provide valid options for ASDII closure, the former being the preferred method.

evidence.reference_title: Transcatheter closure of atrial septal defects.
evidence.snippet: A major reason for this is the lower morbidity of transcatheter closure procedures.
